does: This paper reframes the value alignment problem for AI as fundamentally a structural governance challenge rather than a purely technical issue. Using principal-agent theory from economics, it decomposes misalignment into three interacting axes: objectives (how goals are specified), information (how knowledge is distributed), and principals (whose interests are represented). The work demonstrates that alignment is not a single technical property achievable through model engineering alone, but rather an emergent outcome of how these three dimensions interact in practice. This three-axis framework provides a diagnostic tool for understanding why misalignment occurs in real-world deployed systems and clarifies that alignment success depends on governance structures, not just algorithmic solutions.
connects: This work builds on longstanding principal-agent frameworks in economics and organizational theory, applying them to AI governance for the first time as a comprehensive lens. It responds to limitations in existing AI alignment literature that treats the problem as abstract or deferred to hypothetical superintelligent systems, rather than addressing current deployment challenges. The paper engages with debates about whether alignment is primarily a technical or normative problem, and connects to growing recognition in AI policy literature that alignment outcomes depend on institutional design and stakeholder representation. It extends recent work on AI governance and multi-stakeholder perspectives on AI development, grounding these insights in formal economic theory rather than purely philosophical analysis.
enables: This governance-centric framing enables new approaches to diagnosing and solving alignment failures in deployed systems by examining which objectives are prioritized, how information asymmetries enable or prevent misalignment, and whose values are structurally excluded from decision-making. It points toward alignment research that engages with organizational design, regulatory structures, and accountability mechanisms as core technical challenges rather than peripheral concerns. The framework supports development of governance interventions targeting each axis independently or in combination, opening pathways for multidisciplinary alignment work spanning computer science, economics, policy, and organizational theory. This perspective also enables better evaluation of when technical solutions alone are insufficient and what institutional changes must accompany them.
